subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Account for the following.

  1. The boiling points of ethers are lower than isomeric alcohols.
  2. Ortho-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-methoxy phenol.
  3. Preparation of ethers by acid dehydration of secondary or tertiary alcohols is not a suitable method.
question_text: Account for the following. The boiling points of ethers are lower than isomeric alcohols. Ortho-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-methoxy phenol. Preparation of ethers by acid dehydration of secondary or tertiary alcohols is not a suitable method.
explanation: Boiling point is directly proportional to the intermolecular forces existing in a compound. Ethers have low polarity because they do not show any association by intermolecular hydrogen bonding. On the other hand, their isomeric alcohols have strong H-bonding and so their boiling points are high. Due to strong-R and -I-effect of the NO 2 group, electron density in the O-H bond decreases and hence, the loss of a proton becomes easy. Further, the o-nitrophenoxide ion left after the loss of a proton is stabilised by resonance. Thereby making o-nitrophenol a stronger acid. In contrast, due to +R-effect of -OCH 3 group, the electron density in the O-H bond increases thereby making the loss of a proton difficult. Further more the o-methoxyphenoxide ion left after the loss of a proton is destablised by resonance because the two negative charges repel each other thereby making o-methoxy phenol a weaker acid. Thus, o-nitrophenol is more acidic than o-methoxyphenol. Secondary and tertiary alcohols on dehydration lead to the formation of alkene and not ethers due to extra stability of 2° and 3°carbocation. Hence, not suitable.
solution: Boiling point is directly proportional to the intermolecular forces existing in a compound. Ethers have low polarity because they do not show any association by intermolecular hydrogen bonding. On the other hand, their isomeric alcohols have strong H-bonding and so their boiling points are high. Due to strong-R and -I-effect of the NO 2 group, electron density in the O-H bond decreases and hence, the loss of a proton becomes easy. Further, the o-nitrophenoxide ion left after the loss of a proton is stabilised by resonance. Thereby making o-nitrophenol a stronger acid. In contrast, due to +R-effect of -OCH 3 group, the electron density in the O-H bond increases thereby making the loss of a proton difficult. Further more the o-methoxyphenoxide ion left after the loss of a proton is destablised by resonance because the two negative charges repel each other thereby making o-methoxy phenol a weaker acid. Thus, o-nitrophenol is more acidic than o-methoxyphenol. Secondary and tertiary alcohols on dehydration lead to the formation of alkene and not ethers due to extra stability of 2° and 3°carbocation. Hence, not suitable.
solution_html:
1. Boiling point is directly proportional to the intermolecular forces existing in a compound. Ethers have low polarity because they do not show any association by intermolecular hydrogen bonding. On the other hand, their isomeric alcohols have strong H-bonding and so their boiling points are high.
  2. Due to strong-R and -I-effect of the NO2 group, electron density in the O-H bond decreases and hence, the loss of a proton becomes easy.

    Further, the o-nitrophenoxide ion left after the loss of a proton is stabilised by resonance. Thereby making o-nitrophenol a stronger acid.


    In contrast, due to +R-effect of -OCH3 group, the electron density in the O-H bond increases thereby making the loss of a proton difficult.

    Further more the o-methoxyphenoxide ion left after the loss of a proton is destablised by resonance because the two negative charges repel each other thereby making o-methoxy phenol a weaker acid. Thus, o-nitrophenol is more acidic than o-methoxyphenol.
  3. Secondary and tertiary alcohols on dehydration lead to the formation of alkene and not ethers due to extra stability of 2° and 3°carbocation. Hence, not suitable.

question_html:
Give one chemical test to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds.

  1. Phenol and benzoic acid.
  2. 1-propanol and 2-propanol.
question_text: Give one chemical test to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds. Phenol and benzoic acid. 1-propanol and 2-propanol.
explanation: Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by Ferric chloride test. Test: Benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured ppt. of ferric benzoate. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a iron-phenol complex giving violet colouration. 1-propanol and 2-propanol can be distinguished by Iodoform test. Test: Propan-2-ol on reaction with sodium hydroxide and I 2 (warm) gives a yellow precipitate of iodoform (CHI 3 ). Propan-1-ol on reaction with sodium hydroxide and I 2 (warm) does not give any precipitate. {tex}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_| }\limits_{{\text{OH}}} {\text{H}}}\limits_{{\text{Propan - 2 - ol}}} + 4{{\text{I}}_2}\xrightarrow{{{\text{NaOH}}}} CHI_3 + CH_3COONa+ 5NaI + 5H_2O{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{OH}}}\limits_{{\text{Propan - 1 - ol}}} + 4{{\text{I}}_2}\xrightarrow{{{\text{NaOH}}}}{/tex} No reaction
solution: Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by Ferric chloride test. Test: Benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured ppt. of ferric benzoate. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a iron-phenol complex giving violet colouration. 1-propanol and 2-propanol can be distinguished by Iodoform test. Test: Propan-2-ol on reaction with sodium hydroxide and I 2 (warm) gives a yellow precipitate of iodoform (CHI 3 ). Propan-1-ol on reaction with sodium hydroxide and I 2 (warm) does not give any precipitate. {tex}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_| }\limits_{{\text{OH}}} {\text{H}}}\limits_{{\text{Propan - 2 - ol}}} + 4{{\text{I}}_2}\xrightarrow{{{\text{NaOH}}}} CHI_3 + CH_3COONa+ 5NaI + 5H_2O{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{OH}}}\limits_{{\text{Propan - 1 - ol}}} + 4{{\text{I}}_2}\xrightarrow{{{\text{NaOH}}}}{/tex} No reaction
solution_html:
1. Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by Ferric chloride test.
    Test:
    Benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl3 to give a buff coloured ppt. of ferric benzoate.

    Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl3 to give a iron-phenol complex giving violet colouration.
  2. 1-propanol and 2-propanol can be distinguished by Iodoform test.
    Test:
    Propan-2-ol on reaction with sodium hydroxide and I2 (warm) gives a yellow precipitate of iodoform (CHI3).
    Propan-1-ol on reaction with sodium hydroxide and I2 (warm) does not give any precipitate.
    {tex}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_| }\limits_{{\text{OH}}} {\text{H}}}\limits_{{\text{Propan - 2 - ol}}} + 4{{\text{I}}_2}\xrightarrow{{{\text{NaOH}}}} CHI_3 + CH_3COONa+ 5NaI + 5H_2O{/tex}
    {tex}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{OH}}}\limits_{{\text{Propan - 1 - ol}}} + 4{{\text{I}}_2}\xrightarrow{{{\text{NaOH}}}}{/tex} No reaction

question_html: How is 1-propoxypropane synthesised from propan-1-ol? Write mechanism of this reaction.
question_text: How is 1-propoxypropane synthesised from propan-1-ol? Write mechanism of this reaction.
explanation: 1-propoxypropane can be synthesized from propan-1-ol by dehydration. Propan-1-ol undergoes dehydration in the presence of protic acids (such as H 2 SO 4 , H 3 PO 4 to give 1-propoxypropane. {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}}\limits_{{\text{Propane - 1 - ol}}} - OH\xrightarrow{{{H^ + }}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}}\limits_{{\text{1 - Propoxypropane}}} - O - C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_3}{/tex} The mechanism of this reaction involves the following three steps: Step 1: Protonation {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}}\limits_{Popan - 1 - ol} - \underset{\raise0.3em\hbox{$\smash{\scriptscriptstyle\cdot\cdot}$}}{\ddot O} - {H^ + } \to C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2} - \mathop {{{\underset{\raise0.3em\hbox{$\smash{\scriptscriptstyle\cdot\cdot}$}}{\ddot O} }^ + }}\limits^H - H{/tex} Step 2: Nucleophilic attack Step 3: Deprotonation
solution: 1-propoxypropane can be synthesized from propan-1-ol by dehydration. Propan-1-ol undergoes dehydration in the presence of protic acids (such as H 2 SO 4 , H 3 PO 4 to give 1-propoxypropane. {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}}\limits_{{\text{Propane - 1 - ol}}} - OH\xrightarrow{{{H^ + }}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}}\limits_{{\text{1 - Propoxypropane}}} - O - C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_3}{/tex} The mechanism of this reaction involves the following three steps: Step 1: Protonation {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}}\limits_{Popan - 1 - ol} - \underset{\raise0.3em\hbox{$\smash{\scriptscriptstyle\cdot\cdot}$}}{\ddot O} - {H^ + } \to C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2} - \mathop {{{\underset{\raise0.3em\hbox{$\smash{\scriptscriptstyle\cdot\cdot}$}}{\ddot O} }^ + }}\limits^H - H{/tex} Step 2: Nucleophilic attack Step 3: Deprotonation

1-propoxypropane can be synthesized from propan-1-ol by dehydration.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Phenyl methyl ether reacts with HI to give phenol and methyl iodide and not iodobenzene and methyl alcohol. Why?
question_text: Phenyl methyl ether reacts with HI to give phenol and methyl iodide and not iodobenzene and methyl alcohol. Why?
explanation: Protonation of anisole (phenylmethyl ether) gives methyl phenyl oxonium ion. {tex}{C_6}{H_5} - \mathop O\limits_{\mathop |\limits_H }^ + - C{H_3}{/tex} In this ion, O-CH 3 bond is weaker than O-C 6 H 5 due to partial double bond character because of resonance between the lone pair of electrons of O-atom along with sp 2 -hybridised C-atom of the phenyl ring. Therefore, attack by I - ion exclusively breaks the weaker O-CH 3 bond forming methyl iodide and phenol. The phenol formed does not react further to give aryl halides.
solution: Protonation of anisole (phenylmethyl ether) gives methyl phenyl oxonium ion. {tex}{C_6}{H_5} - \mathop O\limits_{\mathop |\limits_H }^ + - C{H_3}{/tex} In this ion, O-CH 3 bond is weaker than O-C 6 H 5 due to partial double bond character because of resonance between the lone pair of electrons of O-atom along with sp 2 -hybridised C-atom of the phenyl ring. Therefore, attack by I - ion exclusively breaks the weaker O-CH 3 bond forming methyl iodide and phenol. The phenol formed does not react further to give aryl halides.
solution_html:
Protonation of anisole (phenylmethyl ether) gives methyl phenyl oxonium ion.
{tex}{C_6}{H_5} - \mathop O\limits_{\mathop |\limits_H }^ + - C{H_3}{/tex}
In this ion, O-CH3 bond is weaker than O-C6H5 due to partial double bond character because of resonance between the lone pair of electrons of O-atom along with sp2-hybridised C-atom of the phenyl ring. Therefore, attack by I- ion exclusively breaks the weaker O-CH3 bond forming methyl iodide and phenol. The phenol formed does not react further to give aryl halides.

question_html:
Explain the mechanism of the following reactions:

  1. Addition of Grignard's reagent to the carbonyl group of compound forming an adduct followed by hydrolysis.
  2. Acid catalysed dehydration of an alcohol forming an alkene.
  3. Acid catalysed hydration of an alkene forming an alcohol.
question_text: Explain the mechanism of the following reactions: Addition of Grignard's reagent to the carbonyl group of compound forming an adduct followed by hydrolysis. Acid catalysed dehydration of an alcohol forming an alkene. Acid catalysed hydration of an alkene forming an alcohol.
explanation: The first step of the reaction is nucleophilic addition of Grignard reagent to the carbonyl group to form an adduct. Hydrolysis of the adduct yields an alcohol. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - OH\xrightarrow[{443K}]{{{H^ + }}}C{H_2} = C{H_2} + {H_2}O{/tex} Mechanism This reaction involves the following steps: Step I Formation of protonated alcohol Step II Formation of carbocation Step III Loss of proton to give alkene Following steps are involved in the acid catalysed hydration of an alkene. Step I Protonation of alkene to give carbocation by electrophilic attack of H 3 O+ as shown below: H 2 O + H + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H 3 O + Step II Nucleophilic attack of water on carbocation formed. Step III Deprotonation to give an alcohol.
solution: The first step of the reaction is nucleophilic addition of Grignard reagent to the carbonyl group to form an adduct. Hydrolysis of the adduct yields an alcohol. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - OH\xrightarrow[{443K}]{{{H^ + }}}C{H_2} = C{H_2} + {H_2}O{/tex} Mechanism This reaction involves the following steps: Step I Formation of protonated alcohol Step II Formation of carbocation Step III Loss of proton to give alkene Following steps are involved in the acid catalysed hydration of an alkene. Step I Protonation of alkene to give carbocation by electrophilic attack of H 3 O+ as shown below: H 2 O + H + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H 3 O + Step II Nucleophilic attack of water on carbocation formed. Step III Deprotonation to give an alcohol.
solution_html:
1. The first step of the reaction is nucleophilic addition of Grignard reagent to the carbonyl group to form an adduct. Hydrolysis of the adduct yields an alcohol.
  2. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - OH\xrightarrow[{443K}]{{{H^ + }}}C{H_2} = C{H_2} + {H_2}O{/tex}
    Mechanism This reaction involves the following steps:
    Step I Formation of protonated alcohol

    Step II Formation of carbocation

    Step III Loss of proton to give alkene
  3. Following steps are involved in the acid catalysed hydration of an alkene.
    Step I Protonation of alkene to give carbocation by electrophilic attack of H3O+ as shown below:
    H2O + H+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}H3O+ 

    Step II Nucleophilic attack of water on carbocation formed.

    Step III Deprotonation to give an alcohol.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Explain the following observations.

  1. The boiling point of ethanol is higher than of methoxy methane.
  2. Phenol is more acidic than ethanol.
  3. o and p-nitrophenols are more acidic than phenol.
question_text: Explain the following observations. The boiling point of ethanol is higher than of methoxy methane. Phenol is more acidic than ethanol. o and p-nitrophenols are more acidic than phenol.
explanation: As ethanol exists as associated molecules due to strong intermolecular H-bonding, therefore, high energy is required to break these H-bonds, hence, it has higher boiling point than that of methoxy methane which is not associated with such strong intermolecular H-bonding. The phenoxide ion, produced by the loss of a proton by phenol, is stabilised by resonance due to delocalisation of the negative charge on the benzene ring. In contrast, alkoxide ion, however is not stabilised by resonance. On the other hand, it is further destabilised by positive inductive effect of alkyl group. Resonance of phenoxide ion {tex}ROH \rightleftharpoons \mathop {R{O^ - }}\limits_{Alkoxide\,\,ion} + {H^ + }{/tex} NO 2 being an electron-withdrawing group when present at ortho and para-positions withdraws electron density from benzene ring thereby decreasing the electron density on O-H bond and thus, making the release of proton easier 8. Also, the -NO 2 group intensifies the negative charge of phenoxide ion and thus, stablises it and hence, increases its acidic strength as Therefore, ortho, para nitrophenols are more acidic than phenols.
solution: As ethanol exists as associated molecules due to strong intermolecular H-bonding, therefore, high energy is required to break these H-bonds, hence, it has higher boiling point than that of methoxy methane which is not associated with such strong intermolecular H-bonding. The phenoxide ion, produced by the loss of a proton by phenol, is stabilised by resonance due to delocalisation of the negative charge on the benzene ring. In contrast, alkoxide ion, however is not stabilised by resonance. On the other hand, it is further destabilised by positive inductive effect of alkyl group. Resonance of phenoxide ion {tex}ROH \rightleftharpoons \mathop {R{O^ - }}\limits_{Alkoxide\,\,ion} + {H^ + }{/tex} NO 2 being an electron-withdrawing group when present at ortho and para-positions withdraws electron density from benzene ring thereby decreasing the electron density on O-H bond and thus, making the release of proton easier 8. Also, the -NO 2 group intensifies the negative charge of phenoxide ion and thus, stablises it and hence, increases its acidic strength as Therefore, ortho, para nitrophenols are more acidic than phenols.
solution_html:
1. As ethanol exists as associated molecules due to strong intermolecular H-bonding, therefore, high energy is required to break these H-bonds, hence, it has higher boiling point than that of methoxy methane which is not associated with such strong intermolecular H-bonding.
  2. The phenoxide ion, produced by the loss of a proton by phenol, is stabilised by resonance due to delocalisation of the negative charge on the benzene ring. In contrast, alkoxide ion, however is not stabilised by resonance. On the other hand, it is further destabilised by positive inductive effect of alkyl group.

    Resonance of phenoxide ion

    {tex}ROH \rightleftharpoons \mathop {R{O^ - }}\limits_{Alkoxide\,\,ion} + {H^ + }{/tex}
  3. NO2 being an electron-withdrawing group when present at ortho and para-positions withdraws electron density from benzene ring thereby decreasing the electron density on O-H bond and thus, making the release of proton easier 8.


    Also, the -NO2 group intensifies the negative charge of phenoxide ion and thus, stablises it and hence, increases its acidic strength as 

    Therefore, ortho, para nitrophenols are more acidic than phenols.

question_html:
1. Name the reagents and write the chemical equations for the preparation of the following compounds by Williamson's synthesis:
    1. Ethoxybenzene
    2. 2-Methyl-2-Methoxypropane
  2. Why do phenols not give protonation reaction readily?
question_text: Name the reagents and write the chemical equations for the preparation of the following compounds by Williamson's synthesis: Ethoxybenzene 2-Methyl-2-Methoxypropane Why do phenols not give protonation reaction readily?
explanation: The lone pair on oxygen of O-H in phenol is shared with benzene ring through resonance. So the lone pair is not fully present on oxygen. Hence phenols do not undergo protonation reactions. Electron density on oxygen in phenoxide ion is decreased due to resonance.
solution: The lone pair on oxygen of O-H in phenol is shared with benzene ring through resonance. So the lone pair is not fully present on oxygen. Hence phenols do not undergo protonation reactions. Electron density on oxygen in phenoxide ion is decreased due to resonance.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write the IUPAC name of the following:
  2. Give reason for 
    1. Phenol is a stronger acid than alcohol.
    2. Alcohols are comparatively more soluble in water than the corresponding hydrocarbons.
question_text: Write the IUPAC name of the following: Give reason for Phenol is a stronger acid than alcohol. Alcohols are comparatively more soluble in water than the corresponding hydrocarbons.
explanation: The phenoxide ion, produced by the loss of a proton by phenol, is stabilised by resonance due to delocalisation of the negative charge on the benzene ring. In contrast, alkoxide ion, however is not stabilised by resonance. On the other hand, it is further destabilised by positive inductive effect of alkyl group. Resonance of phenoxide ion {tex}ROH \rightleftharpoons \mathop {R{O^ - }}\limits_{Alkoxide\,\,ion} + {H^ + }{/tex} Due to the presence of polar -OH bond, alcohol molecules can form H-bonds with the water molecules and the water-alcohol interaction becomes stronger than the water-water or alcohol-alcohol interactions. That's why, alcohols are more soluble in water.

That's why, alcohols are more soluble in water.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Why is the preparation of ether by acid catalysed dehydration of secondary alcohol not a suitable method?
question_text: Why is the preparation of ether by acid catalysed dehydration of secondary alcohol not a suitable method?
explanation: 2° and 3°alcoholsare sterically hindered and due to steric hindrance, the nucleophilic attack by the alcohol molecule on the protonated alcohol molecule does not occur. The protonated 2°and 3° alcohols readily lose a molecule of water to form stable 2° and 3°carbocations, which after losing a proton give alkene. Thus, acid catalysed dehydration of 2 o and 3°alcohols give alkenes rather than ethers.
solution: 2° and 3°alcoholsare sterically hindered and due to steric hindrance, the nucleophilic attack by the alcohol molecule on the protonated alcohol molecule does not occur.
